content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 19, 2020

Pat Eklund
Mayor Pro Tempore
City of Novato
36 White Oak Way
Novato, CA 94949

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-20-035

Dear Ms. Eklund:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act and Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹ Please note that we are only
providing advice under Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such
as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code.

        Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) l
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

        We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the County District Attorney's Office,
which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either
entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section
1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other
than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

                                        QUESTION

        Given that you are both the Mayor Pro Tempore of Novato and also an officer of your HOA,
a non-profit corporation, does the Act or Section 1090 prohibit you from taking part in a decision to
approve a contract with the HOA involving the transfer of property maintenance duties?

_______________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-20-035
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Yes, under the Act and Section 1090, you are prohibited from taking part in such a
governmental decision, as it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would result in the
modification of taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to your real property. Additionally, because
no exception to Section 1090 applies, including the "rule of necessity," the City Council is also
prohibited from entering into such a contract with the HOA.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are the Mayor Pro Tempore for the City of Novato. You own a home in a development
called Pointe Marin, which is burdened by a Declaration of Covenants, Conditions and Restrictions
("CC&Rs"). The CC&Rs provide for the creation of the Pointe Marin Association ("HOA") which
was formed and duly organized under the laws of the State of California as a non-profit mutual
benefit corporation. Under the CC&Rs, the HOA is required to discharge certain obligations,
including maintaining the common areas owned by the HOA and located within the area governed
by the HOA, including maintaining improvements located on those common areas.

Under the CC&Rs, a person who purchases a lot in the Pointe Marin development governed
by the CC&Rs automatically becomes a member of the HOA and must pay assessments levied by
the HOA, including annual assessments. Accordingly, upon purchasing your home, which is
governed by the CC&Rs, you automatically became a member of the HOA. You are an
uncompensated officer of the HOA and have not been paid anything by the HOA.

A Mello-Roos Community Facilities District ("CFD") was formed to maintain City of
Novato-owned property and improvements located in the Pointe Marin Development. Under the
CFD, the City maintains those improvements. At some point in the future, the City may consider
entering into an agreement with the HOA, which would transfer some of the City's maintenance
obligations under the CFD to the HOA. Currently, HOA members pay a small property tax to the
City as a result of the City's maintenance. In a subsequent telephone call, you clarified that if the
HOA took over maintenance, HOA members would no longer pay the additional property tax, but
member dues could either increase, decrease, or remain neutral, depending on the maintenance
costs.

ANALYSIS

The Act

Section 87100 of the Act provides that "[n]o public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest." Section 87103 further specifies that "[a] public official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on certain enumerated economic interests,
including sources of income and "[a]ny real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(b).)

File No. A-20-035
Page No. 3

Regulation 18701(a) provides the standard for determining whether a financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable when an official's economic interest is explicitly involved in a decision. The
regulation states, "[a] financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to reasonably
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision
before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6)."

Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(3), "[t]he reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other
than a leasehold interest, is material whenever the governmental decision . . . [w]ould impose,
repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel . . . ."

You are an uncompensated officer of the HOA and, therefore, you do not have a source of
income interest in the HOA. You do, however, have an economic interest in your real property.
Both of those economic interests may be financially affected by changes in the assessments levied
by the HOA. Although you are not certain of the exact outcome of a governmental decision shifting
maintenance duties from the City to the HOA, such a decision would repeal or modify "taxes, fees,
or assessments that apply to the parcel." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(3).) Accordingly, the financial
effect of the decision in question is foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and material under
18702(a)(3) and you may not take part in the decision under the Act.

Section 1090

Section 1090 provides, "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district,
and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in
their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." Section 1090 is
concerned with financial interests, other than noninterests or remote interests that prevent public
officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of
their agencies. (Sigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to
strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial
Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Although Section 1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law
and Attorney General opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as
direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of
pecuniary gain. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga
(1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn.5; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen.
158, 161-162 (2001).)

Section 1091.5 provides that "[a]n officer or employee shall not be deemed to be interested
in a contract if his or her interest is . . . [t]hat of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit, tax-
exempt corporation, which, as one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the body or
board or to which the body or board has a legal obligation to give particular consideration, and
provided further that this interest is noted in its official records." (Section 1091.5(a)(7).)

File No. A-20-035
Page No. 4

Section 1091 provides that "[a]n officer shall not be deemed to be interested in a contract
entered into by a body or board of which the officer is a member within the meaning of this article
if the officer has only a remote interest in the contract and if the fact of that interest is disclosed to
the body or board of which the officer is a member and noted in its official records, and thereafter
the body or board authorizes, approves, or ratifies the contract in good faith by a vote of its
membership sufficient for the purpose without counting the vote or votes of the officer or member
with the remote interest." Included within the definition of "remote interest" is "[t]hat of an officer
or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from taxation pursuant to Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal
Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 501(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 501(c)(5) of the Internal Revenue
Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 501(c)(5)), or a nonprofit corporation, except as provided in paragraph (8) of
subdivision (a) of Section 1091.5." (Section 1091(b)(1).)

Although you are a noncompensated officer of a non-profit corporation, supporting the
functions of the City Council is not a primary purpose of the HOA, and the City Council is not
legally obligated to give the HOA any particular consideration. Therefore, Section 1091.5(a)(7)'s
noninterest exception is not applicable to your interest as an officer of the HOA. And although
Section 1091(b)(1)'s remote interest exception might otherwise be applicable, in any case, you also
have an economic interest in your real property, which would foreseeably be affected by a contract
between the HOA and City involving maintenance duties and property assessments. Accordingly,
Section 1090 prohibits you from taking part in any such contract.

Additionally, we must determine whether the City Council may enter into the contract with
the HOA in light of your interest in the contract. Generally, a member of governing body is
conclusively presumed to have participated in any decision of the governing body. Thus, if one
member of the governing board has an interest in a contract, the governing board may not enter the
contract even if the interested member recuses him or herself from the decision. (Thomson v. Call
(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 649-650.)

In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a
board that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (Dietrick Advice Letter, No. A-15-174; 88
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).) The California Supreme Court has stated, "[t]he rule of
necessity permits a government body to act to carry out its essential functions if no other entity is
competent to do so (Eldridge v. Sierra View Local Hospital Dist., supra, 224 Cal.App.3d at pp.
321-322; see Olson v. Cory (1980) 27 Cal.3d 532, 537 . . .), but it requires all conflicted members to
refrain from any participation. If a quorum is no longer available, the minimum necessary number
of conflicted members may participate, with drawing lots or some other impartial method employed
to select them. (Eldridge, at pp. 322-323.)" (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1097.)
Here, there is nothing to indicate that a contract between the HOA and the City regarding property
maintenance is necessary. Under the CFD, the City has historically maintained the improvements
located in the Pointe Marin Development. No facts indicate that a change in status quo is at all
necessary, let alone to the City's benefit, nor is there anything suggesting that if the City did wish to
transfer maintenance duties, that a transfer to the HOA is one of only a limited number of options.²

² To the extent that additional facts may indicate that a decision to transfer the maintenance to the HOA is
necessary for the proper maintenance of the property or that there are limited viable options for the proper maintenance

File No. A-20-035
Page No. 5

Given the above, Section 1090 would apply to you with respect to, and prohibit the City
Council from entering into, a contract between the City and the HOA regarding maintenance of
improvements located in the Pointe Marin Development.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

Kevin Cornwall

By:    Kevin Cornwall
       Counsel, Legal Division
KMC:aja

other than the HOA, you may wish to seek additional advice regarding the potential application of the "rule of
necessity."

sections.analysis:
The Act 
 
 
Section 87100 of the Act provides that “[n]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest.” Section 87103 further specifies that “[a] public official has a financial interest in a 
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will 
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the

including sources of income and “[a]ny real property in which the public official has a direct or 
indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.” (Section 87103(b).)  
 
 
 
 
Regulation 18701(a) provides the standard for determining whether a financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable when an official’s economic interest is explicitly involved in a decision. The 
regulation states, “[a] financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision 
before the official or the official’s agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the 
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).” 
 
 
Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(3), “[t]he reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other 
than a leasehold interest, is material whenever the governmental decision . . . [w]ould impose, 
repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel . . . .” 
 
 
 
You are an uncompensated officer of the HOA and, therefore, you do not have a source of 
income interest in the HOA. You do, however, have an economic interest in your real property. 
Both of those economic interests may be financially affected by changes in the assessments levied

effect of the decision in question is foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and material under 
18702(a)(3) and you may not take part in the decision under the Act.   
 
Section 1090 
 
 
Section 1090 provides, “[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, 
and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in 
their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members.” Section 1090 is 
concerned with financial interests, other than noninterests or remote interests that prevent public 
officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of 
their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended “not only to 
strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety.” (City of Imperial 
Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
 
 
Although Section 1090 does not specifically define the term “financial interest,” case law 
and Attorney General opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as 
direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of 
pecuniary gain. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga 
(1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn.5; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
158, 161-162 (2001).) 
 
 
Section 1091.5 provides that “[a]n officer or employee shall not be deemed to be interested 
in a contract if his or her interest is . . . [t]hat of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit, tax-
exempt corporation, which, as one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the body or 
board or to which the body or board has a legal obligation to give particular consideration, and 
provided further that this interest is noted in its official records.” (Section 1091.5(a)(7).)  
 
 
 
 
Section 1091 provides that “[a]n officer shall not be deemed to be interested in a contract 
entered into by a body or board of which the officer is a member within the meaning of this article 
if the officer has only a remote interest in the contract and if the fact of that interest is disclosed to 
the body or board of which the officer is a member and noted in its official records, and thereafter 
the body or board authorizes, approves, or ratifies the contract in good faith by a vote of its 
membership sufficient for the purpose without counting the vote or votes of the officer or member 
with the remote interest.” Included within the definition of “remote interest” is “[t]hat of an officer 
or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from taxation pursuant to Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal 
Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 501(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 501(c)(5) of the Internal Revenue 
Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 501(c)(5)), or a nonprofit corporation, except as provided in paragraph (8) of 
subdivision (a) of Section 1091.5.” (Section 1091(b)(1).) 
 
 
Although you are a noncompensated officer of a non-profit corporation, supporting the 
functions of the City Council is not a primary purpose of the HOA, and the City Council is not 
legally obligated to give the HOA any particular consideration. Therefore, Section 1091.5(a)(7)’s 
noninterest exception is not applicable to your interest as an officer of the HOA. And although

have an economic interest in your real property, which would foreseeably be affected by a contract 
between the HOA and City involving maintenance duties and property assessments. Accordingly, 
Section 1090 prohibits you from taking part in any such contract.  
 
 
Additionally, we must determine whether the City Council may enter into the contract with 
the HOA in light of your interest in the contract. Generally, a member of governing body is 
conclusively presumed to have participated in any decision of the governing body. Thus, if one 
member of the governing board has an interest in a contract, the governing board may not enter the 
contract even if the interested member recuses him or herself from the decision. (Thomson v. Call 
(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 649-650.)

contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (Dietrick Advice Letter, No. A-15-174; 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).) The California Supreme Court has stated, “[t]he rule of 
necessity permits a government body to act to carry out its essential functions if no other entity is 
competent to do so (Eldridge v. Sierra View Local Hospital Dist., supra, 224 Cal.App.3d at pp. 
321-322; see Olson v. Cory (1980) 27 Cal.3d 532, 537 . . .), but it requires all conflicted members to 
refrain from any participation. If a quorum is no longer available, the minimum necessary number 
of conflicted members may participate, with drawing lots or some other impartial method employed 
to select them. (Eldridge, at pp. 322-323.)” (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1097.) 
Here, there is nothing to indicate that a contract between the HOA and the City regarding property 
maintenance is necessary. Under the CFD, the City has historically maintained the improvements 
located in the Pointe Marin Development. No facts indicate that a change in status quo is at all 
necessary, let alone to the City’s benefit, nor is there anything suggesting that if the City did wish to 
transfer maintenance duties, that a transfer to the HOA is one of only a limited number of options.2 
 
2 To the extent that additional facts may indicate that a decision to transfer the maintenance to the HOA is 
necessary for the proper maintenance of the property or that there are limited viable options for the proper maintenance  
 
 
 
 
Given the above, Section 1090 would apply to you with respect to, and prohibit the City 
Council from entering into, a contract between the City and the HOA regarding maintenance of 
improvements located in the Pointe Marin Development.

a non-profit corporation, does the Act or Section 1090 prohibit you from taking part in a decision to 
approve a contract with the HOA involving the transfer of property maintenance duties?

CONCLUSION: Yes, under the Act and Section 1090, you are prohibited from taking part in such a 
governmental decision, as it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would result in the 
modification of taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to your real property. Additionally, because 
no exception to Section 1090 applies, including the “rule of necessity,” the City Council is also 
prohibited from entering into such a contract with the HOA.
